topics.title: Basic Types & Type Annotations
topics.description: Learn TypeScript's primitive types and how to annotate variables and function signatures.

lessons.title: TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript
lessons.body: Any valid JavaScript is valid TypeScript. TypeScript adds optional type annotations and catches type errors at compile time rather than at runtime.

lessons.title: Primitive types
lessons.body: The basic types are number, string, boolean, null, and undefined. Annotate with a colon after the variable name.

lessons.title: Type inference
lessons.body: TypeScript infers types automatically when you initialise a variable. You only need to annotate when inference is insufficient.
lessons.code:
let count = 0;        // inferred: number
count = "hello";      // Error: Type 'string' is not assignable to type 'number'

lessons.title: Function signatures
lessons.body: Annotate both parameter types and the return type to document intent and catch mistakes.

topics.title: Interfaces & Type Aliases
topics.description: Define the shape of objects using interfaces and type aliases.

lessons.title: interface
lessons.body: An interface describes the shape of an object. It can have optional properties (?) and readonly modifiers.

lessons.title: type alias
lessons.body: type aliases can describe any type — not just objects. They're interchangeable with interfaces for object shapes in most cases.
lessons.code:
type Point = { x: number; y: number };
type ID = string | number;

lessons.title: Extending interfaces
lessons.body: Use extends to build on an existing interface. Composition over repetition.

topics.title: Generics
topics.description: Write reusable, type-safe functions and classes using TypeScript generics.

lessons.title: Why generics?
lessons.body: Generics let you write a function once that works with many types while still preserving type information. The alternative — using any — loses all type safety.

lessons.title: Generic functions
lessons.body: Declare a type parameter in angle brackets. TypeScript infers it from the argument at the call site.

lessons.title: Constraints with extends
lessons.body: Use extends to restrict what types a parameter can be.

lessons.title: Generic interfaces
lessons.body: Interfaces can also be generic, useful for API responses and containers.

topics.title: Conditional & Mapped Types
topics.description: Use TypeScript's type-level programming features to transform and derive types from other types.

lessons.title: Conditional types
lessons.body: T extends U ? X : Y resolves to X if T is assignable to U, otherwise Y. This enables powerful type inference.

lessons.title: infer keyword
lessons.body: infer introduces a type variable inside a conditional type that TypeScript fills in during inference.
lessons.code:
type ReturnType<T> = T extends (...args: unknown[]) => infer R ? R : never;
type N = ReturnType<() => number>; // number

lessons.title: Mapped types
lessons.body: Mapped types iterate over a union of keys and transform each property.
lessons.code:
type Partial<T> = { [K in keyof T]?: T[K] };
type Required<T> = { [K in keyof T]-?: T[K] };

lessons.title: Template literal types
lessons.body: TypeScript can build string union types using template literals, enabling event-name generation and more.
lessons.code:
type EventName<T extends string> = `on${Capitalize<T>}`;
type ClickEvent = EventName<"click">; // 'onClick'
